chapters.title: Depression and anxiety respond to similar fixes
chapters.text:
Anxiety and depression rise and fall together. A psychology professor named Robert Kohlenberg has pointed out that the data does not show two separate conditions with different causes. The National Institutes of Health, the main agency for medical research in the United States, has even stopped funding studies that try to draw a hard line between depression and anxiety. The two are not identical, but they are closely linked. They look like different bands playing their own version of the same song.

The standard medical approach to mental health pushes people toward pills that adjust brain chemistry.

Antidepressants are everywhere. About one in five adults in the United States takes at least one psychiatric medication. Close to one in four middle-aged women in the U.S. is on antidepressants at any given time. Roughly one in ten boys in American high schools takes a strong stimulant to help them focus.

The Western world is swimming in antidepressants. In France, for example, about one in three people is taking a legal mood-altering drug like an antidepressant right now. The U.K. is close behind, with some of the highest use in Europe.

Lost Connections points to scientific studies that challenge common views on depression and anxiety. You will see that the main drivers of rising depression and anxiety live in our environment and in how we relate to it. There are at least nine well-known causes of depression and anxiety, and many of them are on the rise, which leaves us feeling worse.

There are two kinds of antidepressants, the real ones and the chemical ones. The chemical kind gives a short escape that leads to more pain later. The real kind opens a path out of pain. It is not something you buy or swallow. It questions our current explanations for depression and anxiety, shows us the actual causes, and offers lasting solutions.

chapters.title: Profit beats healing in depression and anxiety care
chapters.text:
Big Pharma and most doctors share the same message about depression and anxiety. The problem is in your head, and you need fixing. Then they hook you on antidepressants. The practice has pulled in over one hundred billion dollars so far. The diagnosis is deeply flawed, yet it sticks around because it pays well for the drug companies.

The side effects of antidepressants often hurt the body more than they help the original problem.

People like Peter Kramer, a therapist who believed so strongly in antidepressants that he never took a cent from drug companies, have tried to argue in their favor. Yet the data lines up against those arguments. Kramer's views rest on three claims. The drugs need enough time to kick in. The drugs clearly work for severely depressed people. The trials were heavily incentivized to give companies the answers they wanted.

The first claim collapses when studies run the drugs for more than eight weeks and placebos match or beat the antidepressants. The second claim is undermined by the fact that the studies disproving the chemical imbalance theory were run mostly on people with severe depression. The third claim turns on itself. If a trial is bought by incentives, its results have to be tossed, yet those same results are what get a drug FDA approval. With that, the case for antidepressants as a cure for depression and anxiety falls apart.

Scientists often say antidepressants correct a chemical imbalance in the brain. The flaw is that depression is a social problem before it is a brain problem.

chapters.title: The way we diagnose depression is broken
chapters.text:
Clinicians who treat depression and anxiety follow specific rules laid out in the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual (DSM), now in its fifth edition. The manual is written by panels of psychiatrists and acts as the main guide for nearly every U.S. general practitioner diagnosing depression or anxiety. Its reach stretches around the world.

Joanne Cacciatore is a professor of social work at Arizona State University who specializes in traumatic bereavement. She found that, by DSM criteria, most people in mourning show signs that look like depression and anxiety. The manual's authors then built in what they called the grief exception, but they put a deadline on those symptoms and recommended a depression diagnosis for anyone who kept showing them past that deadline. Cacciatore saw this as an insult to grief, to the bond with the person who died, and to love itself.

Putting a time limit on how long you are allowed to grieve the loss of a loved one is to deny a core part of being human.

The grief exception hints that people can show depression symptoms without being clinically depressed. The deeper question is why losing a loved one is the only life event where depression is treated as a normal reaction.

Other life events, like being stuck in a job you hate or going through a long marriage that ends in divorce, also deserve careful thought. Cacciatore argues our culture makes a basic mistake about pain by ignoring context. If we treat depression only as a brain problem, we lose sight of the real causes and misdiagnose the condition. There are too many causes of depression for antidepressants to be the right answer in every case.

The whole framework would need an overhaul if we built people's life context into how we diagnose and treat depression and anxiety. The conversation would shift from mental health to emotional health.

To be formally diagnosed with depression, a person has to show at least five of nine symptoms listed in the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual on most days.

chapters.title: Setting shapes the real roots
chapters.text:
Where does depression actually start? Anthropologist George Brown tried to crack that question at a psychiatric clinic in South London. The field was split into two camps. Freud's followers believed in the value of looking at each patient's life. Others focused on the patient's biology, especially the brain. That split led to two labels for depression. Reactive depression was tied to life experience, and endogenous depression was blamed on brain malfunction.

Before the 1970s, when Brown started his research, the triggers for reactive depression, or the line between the two types, if the line was even real, were fuzzy. A careful scientific look at patients with depression or high anxiety was long overdue.

Brown and his team interviewed 114 women who had been diagnosed with depression by their psychiatrists, and 344 women from the same neighborhood, Camberwell, who had not been diagnosed. All the women were in the same income band. The goal was to map the women's lives in the year leading up to their depression. The team also wanted to study other factors that might shape mental health over time, beyond single events, and they sorted those into two buckets, difficulties and stabilizers. Difficulties were ongoing chronic problems. Stabilizers were things that could lift mood and protect against despair.

Answering hardship with clinical depression is a natural reaction.

The research showed that people in poverty were more likely to slide into depression. They faced longer stress, more painful life events, and less access to stabilizers. Among people who were not poor, long exposure to severe stress or traumatic events often led to a deep sense of hopelessness.

The team saw the same pattern for anxiety. It came from life going wrong, not from the brain going wrong. No creature exists apart from its setting. The profits from antidepressants buried those results.

chapters.title: Three layers feed depression and anxiety
chapters.text:
The opening that Brown's team created became impossible to dismiss in academic and scientific circles, and the work eventually made its way into psychiatric training in many parts of the Western world. Out of that shift grew the bio-psycho-social model, which welcomes all three layers as possible drivers of depression and anxiety. A pure chemical imbalance in the brain does not explain the full picture. Brushing past the social and psychological roots has let these conditions spread into a pandemic. In fact, the biological layer seems to need the psycho-social layer underneath it. Put differently, when a person's surroundings offer the support they need, depression and anxiety are less likely to take hold.

Caring for depression and anxiety means tuning in to the social world around the patient.

On World Health Day in 2017, the United Nations put out a statement saying the data behind the chemical imbalance theory does not capture the full truth. The statement argued that the story told by the scientific community has actually hurt people's health by skimming the surface. It did not call for an outright ban on medication, but it asked for a deeper look into the real causes. Before a brain chemistry imbalance shows up, an imbalance of power had to appear in someone's life first.

You are not a machine with broken parts. You are an animal whose needs are not being met, in Johann Hari's words.

So how do we tune in to a person's environment when diagnosing depression and anxiety? These big questions still feel too radical for most systems to handle, and they remain on the shelf today.

chapters.title: Lost connections are the real drivers of depression and anxiety
chapters.text:
All the social and psychological causes of depression and anxiety share something in common. They are all forms of disconnection, basic needs lost for one reason or another. Nine of those disconnections are disconnection from meaningful work, from other people, from meaningful values, from a safe childhood, from esteem and recognition, from nature, from a hopeful or secure outlook, and the real influence of genetics and brain changes.

Spending the day on repetitive work makes it hard to connect with the people you love in the evening.

A 2011 to 2012 Gallup survey looked at how workers felt about their jobs around the world. The study covered millions of employees from 142 countries. Only thirteen percent were engaged, meaning they felt a spark for their work, were devoted to their duties, and added to their organization in a positive way. Sixty-three percent were not engaged, basically sleepwalking through their day, putting in time but no energy. Another twenty-four percent were actively disengaged. The misery these workers feel at work spills into how they act at home. They do things that drag down their colleagues and can even hurt their own company.

Today, from the moment we wake up until we sleep, we are busy with work. The idea of fixed working hours has nearly disappeared for most people. So this activity, which eighty-seven percent of us find dull, fills most of our waking hours. Could that be part of why depression and anxiety keep climbing?

chapters.title: Rekindle what you have lost
chapters.text:
Tackling depression and anxiety takes more than solo effort. When the social world of a depressed person shifts for the better, the depression often lifts. Reconnections are a different kind of medicine. Early research points to seven kinds of reconnections that may start to ease depression and anxiety. Think of them as social or psychological antidepressants. We need to bridge back to other people, to social prescribing, to work that matters, to values that matter, to sympathetic joy and a way out of self-absorption, to facing and healing childhood wounds, and to restoring a sense of the future.

These seven paths are early steps grounded in growing research, and they can serve as guideposts. Studying them closely might reveal a new road to take. The reconnections feel bold, because they call for big shifts in our lives and our communities. That ask can feel heavy in a time when our collective power for change feels smaller than it used to be. Still, big problems are not impossible ones.

You will find release from your pain when you pour yourself into a cause larger than yourself.

The individualistic culture of the Western world makes happiness harder to come by. In many Asian societies, where life is more communal, people find it easier to feel glad. Knowing you are not alone in your highs and lows changes how you feel and how you respond to life.

chapters.title: Mental health is a group project, not a solo one
chapters.text:
Coming together, not pulling apart, is what helps people heal. You are not a gadget with broken bits, but a person with real needs, and most of those needs go unmet. A community is not optional. Pile hot coals together and they glow. Yank one out and it goes dark in a hurry. Lives work the same way. Connection keeps us lit, while isolation dims us. Living by values that run deeper than the usual markers is essential. Believing that more shopping or a bigger paycheck brings happiness only cranks up anxiety and sadness. Each of us also needs labor that feels worthwhile, time in nature, a sense of being seen and valued, a stable tomorrow, and genuine ties to other people. The lasting sting of past cruelty has to ease for any of this to take root.

We have grown skilled at feeding the body. We still starve the inner life.

Your brain chemistry is not the villain. The mismatch lives in the social and spiritual world around you. Society, not a missing neurotransmitter, drives the crisis. Hurt, not grey matter, is the real cause. The brain can amplify the trouble, yet it does not start it. Neither the map nor the way out of anxiety and depression sits inside the skull.

The measure of wellness is not how comfortably you fit a broken world, in Jiddu Krishnamurti's view.

It is past time we looked past the obvious. Treating sadness as a head problem, rather than a social and psychological one, traps people in a cycle of fast patches like pills. Those who can shape the right social and psychological space for themselves will taste real freedom from these struggles.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
We stand at a crossroads. We can try to muffle the signal or we can listen to it. Silencing depression and anxiety can cost us years, because the pain keeps coming. Listening to the signal, and letting it guide us away from what harms us, can ease the suffering. We will move toward the things that meet our real needs.

It is easy to blame Big Pharma for the lies it has sold us for decades, but the deeper culprit lies in a trend in our culture. Long before antidepressants existed, we started drifting away from a sense of community and from a value system that puts collective well-being above personal gain. Our isolated, tech-rich lifestyle makes it hard to believe we need society, a strange idea to a 21st-century kid. We have lost sight of the fact that not every problem can be fixed with a purchase.

Even if we act like we live outside a society, our hunger for connection always gives us away. We need to stop seeing depression and anxiety as a kind of madness and start seeing them as the sanest response we have, a signal that our way of living does not have to be this way, and that without help we risk missing the richness of being human.

If your job is stressing you out, look around your environment and try volunteering or joining some form of community service. Notice how that makes you feel over the next couple of weeks.
